skill.name: azure-ai-projects-ts
skill.description: Guides TypeScript developers using the Azure AI Projects SDK for Azure AI Foundry project clients, agents, connections, deployments, datasets, indexes, evaluations, and OpenAI client access.
skill.summary: Build Azure AI Foundry applications with the Azure AI Projects TypeScript SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and blocker patterns, but review found they are Markdown code fences, inline code, or ordinary TypeScript examples, not executable skill code. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it documents Azure network calls, local dataset uploads, and a credential-returning connection API that users must handle carefully.

medium_findings.title: Credential-Bearing Connection Example
medium_findings.description: The skill documents getWithCredentials for Azure project connections. This is a legitimate SDK capability, but generated code using it may expose connection secrets if logged, stored, or sent to other services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented API name directly indicates credential retrieval, and the surrounding section is about Azure connections. The file is documentation rather than executable code, so the risk depends on how generated examples are used.

medium_findings.title: Local Dataset Upload Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill shows uploading a local file and folder to Azure datasets. This is expected for dataset workflows, but users could accidentally upload sensitive local data without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly show local file and folder paths passed to dataset upload APIs. The behavior is legitimate for Azure AI Projects, but it has clear data handling implications.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline code markers. The skill contains examples, not Ruby backtick execution or runnable shell logic beyond installation snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows the reported locations are fenced Markdown, table inline code, or TypeScript examples. No Ruby code or dynamic shell execution is present in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Examples Are Placeholders
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are an Azure endpoint placeholder and an example MCP server URL. They do not send data by themselves and are part of user-facing setup documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The first URL contains placeholder segments, and the second uses example.com. I found no code that automatically contacts either URL from the skill file.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access Is Configuration Only
low_findings.description: The process.env example reads the Azure AI Project endpoint for client initialization. I found no evidence of environment file parsing or secret exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is used as a service endpoint in a TypeScript sample. There is no .env file access, credential enumeration, or outbound transfer of environment contents.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Blockers Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm and system reconnaissance matches do not correspond to cryptographic or host reconnaissance operations in context. They appear to be substring matches inside normal prose or SDK sample fields.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain the skill description, a weather function description, and an agent response body. I found no weak hash, cipher usage, or system enumeration command.

low_findings.title: Critical Combined Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The dangerous combination heuristic depends on command execution, network access, and credential access. Review found no executable skill code or automatic exfiltration path, so the critical finding is dismissed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The combined heuristic was triggered by individually weak signals in a documentation file. A credential-related SDK method is documented, but there is no malicious control flow or network exfiltration logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Retrieval API Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents retrieving Azure connection credentials. This pattern should be used only when the user explicitly needs credentials and can protect them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The API name and connection section confirm credential retrieval semantics. The skill does not itself execute the call, so the risk is contextual.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Upload From Local Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents uploading local files and folders to Azure datasets. Generated code should include user confirmation and data classification checks before upload.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited upload methods and local paths are explicit. This is normal SDK usage, but it can move local data into cloud storage.

content.user_title: Build Azure AI Foundry TypeScript Apps
content.value_statement: Azure AI Foundry developers need accurate SDK patterns for agents, connections, deployments, datasets, and indexes. This skill provides focused TypeScript guidance for Azure AI Projects workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install @azure/ai-projects and @azure/identity for TypeScript projects.
Shows how to initialize AIProjectClient with DefaultAzureCredential and an Azure project endpoint.
Provides examples for creating Azure AI Foundry agents with tools such as file search, web search, functions, and MCP.
Documents how to use getOpenAIClient for responses and conversations through a project client.
Covers listing and retrieving connections, deployments, datasets, indexes, evaluators, and memory stores.
Includes dataset upload, index creation, agent cleanup, and deployment filtering patterns.
limitations: It is a Markdown reference skill, not a complete runnable application template.
It does not validate Azure resource permissions, quotas, or project configuration.
It does not include tests, error handling wrappers, or production deployment scripts.
Some examples use placeholder names and must be adapted before use.

use_cases.title: Create an Azure AI Foundry Agent
use_cases.description: Build a TypeScript agent definition with model selection, instructions, and supported tools.

use_cases.title: Inventory Project Resources
use_cases.description: List connections, deployments, datasets, and indexes from an Azure AI Foundry project.

use_cases.title: Prototype Dataset and Search Workflows
use_cases.description: Upload local datasets and configure Azure AI Search indexes for agent retrieval scenarios.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Project Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Azure AI Projects TypeScript client using DefaultAzureCredential and an endpoint from the environment.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a TypeScript example that creates an Azure AI Foundry prompt agent, sends one conversation message, and cleans up the agent.

prompt_templates.title: Add Retrieval Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure an Azure AI Projects TypeScript agent with file search and Azure AI Search tools using existing project resources.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure AI Projects TypeScript plan for agents, deployments, datasets, indexes, credentials, cleanup, and observability risks.

output_examples.input: Create a TypeScript agent with file search.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation outline that initializes the project client, references a vector store, creates a versioned agent, and notes cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: List available Azure OpenAI deployments in my project.
output_examples.output: A guided approach for using the deployments operation group, filtering by publisher, and selecting a model deployment name.

output_examples.input: Upload a local folder as a dataset.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step dataset workflow with version naming, local path review, upload guidance, listing versions, and deletion guidance.

best_practices: Use managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential instead of hardcoded secrets.
Review local files before uploading datasets to Azure project storage.
Delete temporary agents, conversations, datasets, and indexes after experiments.
anti_patterns: Do not log values returned by credential-bearing connection APIs.
Do not paste real endpoints, tenant identifiers, or credentials into prompts.
Do not run dataset upload examples on broad folders without checking their contents.

faq.question: What package does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers the Azure AI Projects SDK for JavaScript and TypeScript, published as @azure/ai-projects.

faq.question: Does it create Azure resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide what code to run against Azure.

faq.question: Can it help with Azure AI Foundry agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for creating versioned agents and configuring common agent tools.

faq.question: Does it include authentication guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows DefaultAzureCredential with an endpoint loaded from an environment variable.

faq.question: Can it help with datasets and indexes?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents dataset upload, dataset version listing, and Azure AI Search index creation.

faq.question: What should I review before using generated code?
faq.answer: Review credentials, local file paths, Azure permissions, cleanup behavior, and any network-connected tools.
